body:
I see. Ya so HF (last time I checked) didn't easily support stop sequences. So one way around this is something like

```
response = manifest.run(prompt, **kwargs, return_response=True)
if stop:
    # Assuming the goal is to get the first stop token
    shortest_ans = sorted([response.get_response(st) for st in stop], key=len)
    ans = shortest_ans[0]
else:
    ans = response.get_response()
return ans
```

body:
Yes that pattern works fine when initialising, it's just annoying when you have to change the callback manager across the board for an existing agent that you don't want to throw away (because eg you want to keep memory)

ie. something like this

```py
class MyLangchainAgent:
    agent_executor: AgentExecutor
    other_langchain_objects: list[Chain | BaseLLM] = []

    def __init__(self, state: dict, loop: asyncio.AbstractEventLoop):
        super().__init__(state, loop)

        llm = OpenAI(temperature=0, verbose=True)
        llm_math_chain = LLMMathChain(llm=llm, verbose=True)
        tools = [
            Tool(
                name="Calculator",
                func=llm_math_chain.run,
                description="useful for when you need to answer questions about math",
            ),
        ]
        self.agent_executor = initialize_agent(
            tools, llm, agent="zero-shot-react-description", verbose=True
        )
        self.langchain_objects = [llm_math_chain]

    async def __call__(self, user_message: dict, ctx: RunContext):
        # Set up callback manager for the run
        callback_manager = CallbackManager([ConcatCallbackHandler(ctx)])
        for obj in self.other_langchain_objects + [
            self.agent_executor,
            self.agent_executor.agent.llm_chain,
            self.agent_executor.agent.llm_chain.llm,
        ]:
            obj.callback_manager = callback_manager

        # Run the agent
        await asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(
            None, self.agent_executor.run, "".join(user_message["content_parts"])
        )
```

body:
Sorry if this is a n00b question - just familiarizing myself here 😄 - but would it make sense to avoid the quadratic run time? 

```python
def _reduce_tokens_below_limit(self, docs: List[Document]) -> List[Document]:

    num_docs = len(docs)
    
    if isinstance(self.combine_documents_chain, StuffDocumentsChain):
        tokens = [self.combine_documents_chain.llm_chain.llm.get_num_tokens(self._page_content(doc)) for doc in docs]

        token_count = sum(tokens[:num_docs])
        while token_count > self.max_tokens_limit:
            num_docs -= 1
            token_count -= tokens[num_docs]

    return docs[:num_docs]
```
author: rowillia

body:
TL;DR: I'll make the change and go with what you recommend.

Though I wanted to share some thoughts on why I went with the recursive function design in the first place,

- This code was something I had implemented in my use case, so readability was crucial for me. I believe recursive code, when done right, is lovely.
- The K value would always be in single or lower double digits, so I didn't have to worry about Stack overflow or other performance issues. 
- E.g., Doing a similarity search in a vector store can be slow, primarily if you use a remote index like Pinecone; I wanted to call them sparingly. That's why I do slicing on the docs list.
- I felt encoding would add a few milliseconds, and given the OpenAI response is usually in higher single-digit seconds, it would be a premature optimization.

Despite that, we must focus on the most performant code when implementing this functionality in a general-purpose library. Because we won't know if K will remain a small value tomorrow for newer LLM models, and most likely, we will have moved to other things by then. 😄 

So thank you so much for taking the time for the suggestions.

**PS**: @rowillia: Yours is better, but the overhead is minimal when the K value is small, especially since addition is a quick operation. Doing encoding again and again, which was my original sin, is more expensive.

body:
It looks to me like this may write the index memory pointer, i.e. the content of the variable, rather than the index content. This would manifest as a failure reading back the correct data from a newly launched process. There are index writing methods at https://github.com/facebookresearch/faiss/wiki/Index-IO,-cloning-and-hyper-parameter-tuning#generic-io-support .
EDIT: note also that faiss does support data larger than fits in ram, although langchain does not use this feature so it could be ignored. involves different saving/loading paradigms but generally uses disk files; see https://github.com/facebookresearch/faiss/wiki/Indexes-that-do-not-fit-in-RAM

body: @nfcampos - It's a hassle to async-ify all vector store implementations so for now I'm running them in threadpool executor to avoid blocking main runloop. However, since vectorstore implementations are not guaranteed to be threadsafe, and with the default executor, you can have multiple threads, I'm not sure this is the best approach. Thoughts here?
author: agola11

body:
Hmm that's an interesting problem. The fundamental issue I think (but correct me if I'm wrong) is that, for maximum throughput, we want eg multiple simultaneous calls into this chain to each call into the vector store and 1. not block other things in the event loop and 2. not get blocked waiting on other vector store calls?

So if we somehow placed the vector store into its own dedicated thread (or something to that effect) that wouldn be enough to solve (1) but not (2) as multiple simultaneous calls to it would have to be queued up?

Looking at each vector store, the ones that do just do http requests I assume could be made async or threadsafe sync trivially. The ones that could be non-trivially not-thread safe are the ones that run locally, so faiss and chroma? Faiss looks to be thread safe for reads (not writes) https://github.com/facebookresearch/faiss/wiki/Threads-and-asynchronous-calls, don't know about chroma

Thoughts?

body:
Hey @stepp1, glad you found my API wrapper! You're right: it's unofficial, but it's actively maintained (and pretty stable).

I have a few suggestions for shortening the `ArXivSearchAPIWrapper` code; I haven't tested them, but I think they should be safe to drop-in. These updates make more direct use of `arxiv`'s built-in generator and result classes (removing an intermediate).

I'll leave it to @hwchase17 and other maintainers to weigh in on whether the wrapper follows typical langchain patterns. Happy to re-review here if you tag me.

In my personal experimentation, I've been using a *very* slim decorated tool:

```python
@tool
def arxiv_search(query: str) -> str:
    """Search arXiv for academic papers relevant the input query."""
    summaries = []
    for result in arxiv.Search(query=query, max_results=10).results():
        summaries.append("{title}\nDate: {date}\nAuthors: {authors}\nSummary: {summary}".format(
            title=result.title,
            date=result.published,
            authors=", ".join([a.name for a in result.authors]),
            summary=result.summary
        ))
    return "\n\n".join(summaries)
```

This obscures a lot of client configuration, but for single-page results sets (when `max_results` ≤ the client's `page_size`, which defaults to 100) the client configuration essentially doesn't matter.
author: lukasschwab

body:
It's nice to have a helper folding the `self` parameters into a search, but I'd leave it up to the caller when/how to resolve the generator.

```suggestion
    def _search(self, query: str) -> Iterator:
        """Construct an arXiv search for query with stored parameters."""
        from arxiv import Search
        return Search(
            query=query,
            max_results=self.max_results,
            sort_by=self.sort_by,
            sort_order=self.sort_order
        )
```
author: lukasschwab

body:
I think you can eliminate much of this code by using the `Search(...).results()` generator directly, rather than wrapping it in a generator (`_parse_results`) converting the results from objects to maps.

If you *do* want to do that conversion, you might consider using the `__dict__ ` built-in: `result. __dict__ `.

Note: this rewrite of `run` assumes the suggestion I made below in this file (`_arxiv_search_results` → `_search`).

```suggestion
def run(self, query: str) -> str:
        """Run query through ArXiv Search and parse result."""
        # papers = self.results(query)

        papers = []
        for paper in self._search(query).results():
            papers.append("\n".join([
                f'Title: {paper.title.strip()}',
                f'Authors: {", ".join([a.name for a in paper.authors])}',
                f'Abstract: {paper.summary.strip()}'
            ]))

        if not papers:
            return "No good arXiv Search result was found"
        
        return "\n\n".join(papers)
```

body:
Love to see this. Thanks for doing this as this was definitely on @tylerhutcherson and I's list. I have a couple high level comments.

1. I think the name ``Redis`` would be better since people might assume ``RediSearch`` is a different thing. First part of the connection could use the ``INFO MODULES`` command to ensure that RediSearch is loaded. Another point is that we allow for vectors to be indexed in RedisJSON which would be confusing if people used the RediSearch container to use both modules without knowing they had to use the Redis-Stack container for this.

2. In relation, I think it would be nice to also have the ability to store as hash or JSON. This, I can do in a follow up PR though.

3. I left some performance pieces in there, but I'm happy to also do these as a follow up.

4. Lots of points on connection arguments. It would be ideal to match the semantics of the REdis-py library so that this is a similar drop in for our community.

5. I didn't see the addition of a optional package like ``pip install langchain[redis]`` which I think would be really nice. pinning a version to ensure these features are present is a must though.

Let me know what you think, we are happy to contribute some of these changes if you get stumped. Please reach out for clarification. 

Awesome!

body:
I'd suggest thinking about how this relates to the self-critique+revision approach from anthropic that someone already added: https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/1147

This `RestrictionGuard` is similar in spirit, but at least 2 downsides:

- it's redundant/overlapping
- it doesn't allow for revision/retry logic

Meanwhile, `ConstitutionalChain` may have some downsides too. eg at first glance, it's `n` LLM calls to evaluate `n` constitutional principles which is not tenable for real/heavy use case. 


Generally tho this is cool! Models evaluating themselves / other models. There's an online validation (+ retry) use case + offline evaluation use case. Should try to converge on something and then write many more examples. Happy to help with.
author: jerwelborn

body:
I agree the Anthropic approach is actually better than what I have implemented for the restriction guard when using it on LLMS. That being said, I think of this as being a separate tool that overlaps in that one area.

The restriction guard can be applied to any function that has text as an output. This means it can be applied to agents, to llms, to chains, or even to user input. It should be seen as a blanket way to restrict text using an LLM- not just a way to restrict LLMs. For example, we may want to use an LLM to restrict a user from asking anything that is off-topic. This is impossible to do with a ConstitutionalChain for obvious reasons. I actually thought about adding more complex retry logic and was planning on it at first but it would prevent this type of use.

One solution could be to edit the docs to make very clear the tradeoffs here when choosing one or the other for LLM guarding. The ConstitutionalChain ought to be the recommended way of doing things for chains. For things like checking user input, agent output, even stuff like cleaning data (maybe you want to remove all fake sounding submitted emails before adding them to your newsletter) the restriction guard is useful.

One other things to note is that the other two guards, @StringGuard and @CustomGuard, are also useful as distinct from the constitutional chain. In particular, the custom guard can be used to pass in any sentiment model. For a heavy use case I anticipate this being far more cost effective than a constitutional chain or restriction guard. A user can train a local sentiment model on the type of response they want to see and run it instead of multiple calls to gpt. See the bottom of security.ipynb for a simple example.

At the end of the day, happy to help change stuff or move it around, but I do think this stuff should be seen as a different set of tools for an overlapping but different set of uses than the constitutional chain. 

Also, though, the prompt for the restriction guard can definitely use some work and wherever we can borrow common logic / code from the constitutional chain (while retaining the llm agnostic approach of the restriction guard: eg no retry logic) we should.

body:
Yeah, although with logic inverted -- allow folks to specify what exceptions can be excepted if they want to rather than have to throw a specific exception for halting execution.

In my mind though AssertionErrors are special and generally not be caught as they indicate there's a logical error in the code and execution should halt.

The use case is to make sure that we don't hide a logical error in the parsing code. So when an exception is raised we can distinguish between parser input being malformed vs. the parser itself having a bug.

I imagine with the agents, things could get interesting as they may try to compensate for the parse bug

body:
How are various forms of validation + retry incorporated. It seems we these 2 cases. Pseudocode:

```
def predict(prompt):
  completion = llm(prompt)
  try:
    parsed_completion = parser(completion)
  except ParserException as e:
    # Handle failures captured by ECF
    if retry_parse:
      retry_completion = generic_retry_thing(prompt, completion)
      parsed_completion = parser(retry_completion)

  completion = parsed_completion
  
  for guardrail in guardrails:
    # Handle failures captured by guardrail
    completion = guardrail.evaluate(prompt, completion)

  return completion
```

body:
I agree that there are too many storage options, but I don't agree that that means that we should not include any here.

Based on that same argument of having too many options what is the reason to have any functionality in this library at all? We could just say that prompt engineering has too many options and just write a documentation on how one can write applications with prompt engineering instead of writing `langchain`.

It makes sense to write things into the library that is expected to be used by many, so they don't all need to re-implement the wheel.

But yes, for those storage options which are very niche, the argument not to include them could be valid.

Redis is definitely not niche, so that should be included but DynamoDB could be considered as niche and not included.

Ps.: I would be curious as to how many users of this library will be able to write their own driver for their own storage option. I would guess that probably less than 30%. :-)

Either way this decision (what to include in the library and what not to) probably belongs to @hwchase17.

```suggestion
    """Base interface for chat message history

    """
    Example:
        .. code-block:: python

            class FileChatMessageHistory(BaseChatMessageHistory):
                storage_path:  str
                session_id: str
               
               @property
               def messages(self):
                   with open(os.path.join(storage_path, session_id), 'r:utf-8') as f:
                       messages = json.loads(f.read())
                    return messages_from_dict(messages)     
                
               def add_user_message(self, message: str):
                   message_ = HumanMessage(content=message)
                   messages = self.messages.append(_message_to_dict(_message))
                   with open(os.path.join(storage_path, session_id), 'w') as f:
                       json.dump(f, messages)
               
               def add_ai_message(self, message: str):
                   message_ = AIMessage(content=message)
                   messages = self.messages.append(_message_to_dict(_message))
                   with open(os.path.join(storage_path, session_id), 'w') as f:
                       json.dump(f, messages)
                       
               def clear(self):
                   with open(os.path.join(storage_path, session_id), 'w') as f:
                       f.write("[]")
    """    

    messages: List[BaseMessage]
```

body:
@hwchase17, @tylerhutcherson ,
What happened to the TTL ([see](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/2122/commits/dd72715410fca61de1e7bcebe0b4551ddba04ceb#diff-7ef42aa53f31305f6c6ea1c5e94084f5164f274e6de1575322e6e61dad018138L72))?
I am fine changing to using a Redis List data type, but it seems we have also dropped the TTL option with this change.

I don't think it is a good idea not to have a TTL, as that means that these records would be kept in Redis forever with no expiration option.

Outside it probably not being a good idea to accumulate data forever in Redis, also most data privacy laws disallow that.

With `lpush` now you would need to set the expiration of the list separately, each time in the append operation,
see https://redis-py.readthedocs.io/en/stable/commands.html#redis.commands.cluster.RedisClusterCommands.expire
author: zoltan-fedor

body:
We should add back the TTL option which got removed by switching to using the Redis list object - as the `lpush` has no TTL option, now the TTL need to be set by a separate call, see https://redis-py.readthedocs.io/en/stable/commands.html#redis.commands.cluster.RedisClusterCommands.expire


Something like
```
if self.ttl:
    self.redis_client.expire(self.key, self.ttl)
```

@hwchase17, @tylerhutcherson , @3coins,
I know this just got released, but trying it out just found a bug with it.

Unfortunately the use of `self.redis_client.lrange(self.key, 0, -1)` returns the messages in the list in a RANDOM ORDER, not in the order how it was inserted.

I haven't yet looked into how to fix this, just wanted to let everybody know. 

CORRECTION: `lrange()` seems to receive the messages in descending order (last added message first), so it seems we just need to sort them in the opposite order, that should be an easy fix. I will make a new PR for that.
See [PR raised](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/2167) to fix this.

body:
Exposing a generator on the callbackhandler via `stream` will work, but the main issue is that with the current API, we don't have control over which LLM will stream tokens. Several chains (like `ConversationalRetrieval`) make multiple calls to the llm, and we'd only want to stream back the results from the last one. I think there are couple things we can do:

1) Add in the `StreamCallbackHandler` that @nfcampos introduced in this diff, but remove the llm parameter in the constructor to the `LangChainPoeHandler` class, replacing with the callback handler. The set-up for streaming is done outside of the `PoeHandler` as it will vary chain to chain. It would look something like this (for `ConversationalRetrievalQA`):
```
# Construct a ConversationalRetrievalChain with a streaming llm for combine docs
# and a separate, non-streaming llm for question generation
llm = OpenAI(temperature=0)
stream_handler = StreamCallbackHandler()
streaming_llm = OpenAI(streaming=True, 
callback_manager=AsyncCallbackManager([stream_handler]), verbose=True, temperature=0)

question_generator = LLMChain(llm=llm, prompt=CONDENSE_QUESTION_PROMPT)
doc_chain = load_qa_chain(streaming_llm, chain_type="stuff", prompt=QA_PROMPT)

qa = ConversationalRetrievalChain(
    retriever=vectorstore.as_retriever(), combine_docs_chain=doc_chain, question_generator=question_generator)

poe_handler = LangChainPoeHandler(qa, stream_handler)
```
In `LangChainPoeHandler.get_response`:
```
async def get_response(self, query):
  run = asyncio.create_task(self.chain.acall(query))

  async for token in this.callback_handler.stream():
      yield token

  await run
```
We can have an `as_poe_handler` to some of the common chains to cut down on this boilerplate.

2) Second, more involved approach which is something we probably don't want to do now but will want to revisit in the future is to have the option to return a generator for tokens on `BaseChain` via a method called `stream` (which is what we have for some LLMs already). Initially, when we implemented streaming, we used callbacks bc it was the easiest solution and didn't require any API changes to chains/etc. However, some users might want more flexibilty and the ability to interact with the raw generator.

body: _should_continue only checks after each iteration is completed.  Suppose you wanted a time limit of 30s but each iteration takes ~5s.  If an iteration finishes at 29s, _should_continue will return True, another iteration will run, and the loop will terminate after ~34s.  With asyncio_timeout, execution will be interrupted after exactly 30s

body:
It may be nice to separate the single doc and the batch doc interfaces

```python

class BaseDocumentProcessor(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def process_document(self, documents: Document) -> Document:
        """Process documents."""

    def process_documents(self, documents: List[Document]) -> List[Document]:
      return [self.process_document(doc) for doc in documents] # <-- This is annoying to implement all the time
```

This would provide a default for loop implementation while allowing inherting classes to over-ride if batching can be made more efficient. 

Batch composition is easy: 

```python
class  SequentialBatchProcessor():  # <-- Only supports batch case, not streaming
   def __init__(self, processors: Sequence[Processors]) -> None:
      self.processors = []

    def process_documents(self, documents: List[Document] | Document) -> List[Document]:
      docs = docs if isinstance(docs, (list, tuple)) else [docs]
      for processor in self.processors:
       docs = processor(docs)
       return docs
```

IMO would be nice to: 
* swap `List` -> `Sequence` as well on inputs
* figure out if it's possible to make a better streaming version (more complicated given that a splitter can be used as a processor with interface of doc -> [doc] or [doc] -> [doc])
